coredata.dc:title: Boron-doped carbon nano dot anchored thin film coating on cobalt vanadium oxide for ultrafast energy storage devices
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Jang, Ha-Na
Jo, Myeong-Hun
Ahn, Hyo-Jin
coredata.dc:description: Lithium-ion capacitors (LICs) with ultrafast rate capabilities are required to increase the applicability of rechargeable energy storage devices. Considering that the power density of an LIC is primarily determined by the anode properties, carbon coating onto the anode material is a promising method of accelerating electron transfer between particles and alleviating structural collapse during repeated charge–discharge cycles. In this study, a new coating configuration featuring a boron-doped carbon nano dot-anchored thin film on cobalt vanadium oxide (BC-CVO) was demonstrated. The coating morphology, wherein the carbon nano dots were connected by a thin carbon film, induced nano-scale surface roughness on CVO, expanding the contact area between the functional groups on the coating layer and electrolyte. The boron-doped carbon lattice structure provided ultrafast electron transfer inside the CVO particles, which allowed reversible electron transfer even under ultrafast charge–discharge cycles. This morphological and chemical combination significantly improved the ultrafast-rate capability of the LIC anodes (429.6 mAh/g at 4000 mA/g and 534.3 mAh/g after 1000 cycles at 2000 mAh/g) compared to that of bare CVO. Furthermore, an LIC full cell fabricated with a BC-CVO anode and an activated carbon cathode showed high energy and power densities.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon coating
Carbon nano dot anchoring
Cobalt vanadium oxide
Ultrafast charge transport kinetics

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of bare CVO, carbon nano dot-anchored thin film coated CVO (C-CVO), and boron-doped carbon nano dot-anchored thin film coated CVO (BC-CVO)

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Korea Ministry of Trade Industry and Energy
Ministry of Science, ICT and Future Planning

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Nano & Material Technology Development Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF, South Korea) funded by Ministry of Science and ICT (2021M3H4A3A02086102) and the Industrial Strategic Technology Development Program (20024261, Development of thick film electrode and cell manufacturing technology for high-performance lithium iron phosphate battery with energy density of over 200 Wh/kg) funded By the Ministry of Trade, Industry & Energy (MOTIE, South Korea).

This work was supported by the Nano & Material Technology Development Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by Ministry of Science and ICT ( 2021M3H4A3A02086102 ) and the Industrial Strategic Technology Development Program ( 20024261 , Development of thick film electrode and cell manufacturing technology for high-performance lithium iron phosphate battery with energy density of over 200 Wh/kg) funded By the Ministry of Trade, Industry & Energy (MOTIE, Korea).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD curves in the 2θ range of 10°−90° and (b) TGA curves of bare CVO, C-CVO, and BC-CVO in the temperature range of 24−900 °C.
Low-magnification SEM images of (a) bare CVO, (b) C-CVO, and (c) BC-CVO, and high-magnification SEM images of (d) bare CVO, (e) C-CVO, and (f) BC-CVO.
(a−c) TEM images and (d) FFT image of bare CVO. (e−g) TEM images and (h) FFT image of BC-CVO.
TEM-EDS mapping image of BC-CVO for C, B, O, Co, and V.

XPS spectra of bare CVO, C-CVO, and BC-CVO: (a) C1s, (b) O1s, (c) Co2p, and (d) V2p spectra. (e) C1s bonding proportion of bare CVO, C-CVO, and BC-CVO. (f) Schematic illustration of the formation mechanism of the carbon nano dot-anchored thin film coating layer on the CVO surface.

Voltage profiles of (a) bare CVO, (b) C-CVO, and (c) BC-CVO electrodes at 100 mA/g. (d) Specific capacity and CE variations according to the stepped current densities of bare CVO, C-CVO, and BC-CVO electrodes. (e) Specific capacity variations after 100 cycles at 100 mA/g. and (f) Specific capacity and CE variations of bare CVO, C-CVO, and BC-CVO electrodes after 1000 cycles at 2000 mA/g.

Schematic diagram of the full-cell configurations of (a) bare CVO//AC and (b) BC-CVO//AC. GCD curves at different current densities: (c) bare CVO//AC and (d) BC-CVO//AC full-cells. Enlarged GCD curves: (e) bare CVO//AC and (f) BC-CVO//AC full-cells.

(a) Specific capacitance and capacity variations of bare CVO//AC and BC-CVO//AC full-cells at different current densities. (b) energy density variations of bare CVO//AC, BC-CVO//AC full-cells, and previous CVO-based full cells at different power densities. Operation of a 1.5 V LED with (c) bare CVO//AC full-cell and (d) BC-CVO//AC full-cell. (e) Enlarged photos in the initial illumination of LEDs by bare CVO//AC and BC-CVO//AC cells.

head.ce:title: Boron-doped carbon nano dot anchored thin film coating on cobalt vanadium oxide for ultrafast energy storage devices

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, Seoul National University of Science and Technology, Seoul 01811, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Materials Science and Engineering
Seoul National University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, Seoul National University of Science and Technology, Seoul 01811, Republic of Korea

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: H.-N. Jang and M.-H. Jo contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Lithium-ion capacitors (LICs) with ultrafast rate capabilities are required to increase the applicability of rechargeable energy storage devices. Considering that the power density of an LIC is primarily determined by the anode properties, carbon coating onto the anode material is a promising method of accelerating electron transfer between particles and alleviating structural collapse during repeated charge–discharge cycles. In this study, a new coating configuration featuring a boron-doped carbon nano dot-anchored thin film on cobalt vanadium oxide (BC-CVO) was demonstrated. The coating morphology, wherein the carbon nano dots were connected by a thin carbon film, induced nano-scale surface roughness on CVO, expanding the contact area between the functional groups on the coating layer and electrolyte. The boron-doped carbon lattice structure provided ultrafast electron transfer inside the CVO particles, which allowed reversible electron transfer even under ultrafast charge–discharge cycles. This morphological and chemical combination significantly improved the ultrafast-rate capability of the LIC anodes (429.6 mAh/g at 4000 mA/g and 534.3 mAh/g after 1000 cycles at 2000 mAh/g) compared to that of bare CVO. Furthermore, an LIC full cell fabricated with a BC-CVO anode and an activated carbon cathode showed high energy and power densities.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Rechargeable energy storage devices are considered indispensable components for powering electronic devices such as electric vehicles, drones, and mobile phones. However, some performance factors, such as energy density, power density, cyclic stability, and flame retardancy, need to be improved to achieve viable operation of these devices. Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) and supercapacitors have been widely used commercially because of their excellent performance. Nevertheless, each device has a different energy storage mechanism, resulting in certain shortcomings in terms of power density (for LIBs) and energy density (for supercapacitors). LIBs can hold large amounts of charge by storing ions through Faradaic reactions such as intercalation, alloying, and conversion, whereas supercapacitors retain charge through physical adsorption on the surfaces of active materials, thus enabling fast ion transport. Lithium-ion capacitors (LICs) have attracted significant attention owing to their high energy and power densities, achieved by bridging the gap between LIBs and supercapacitors. A conventional LIC is composed of a battery-type anode and capacitor-type cathode, providing high energy and power densities, respectively [1–8]. Amatucci et al. [9] first utilized Li4Ti5O12 (LTO) as the anode material and porous activated carbon (AC) as the cathode material. The use of LTO as an anode broadened the anode potential to 0 V and increased the energy density of the LIC. This finding demonstrates the ability of asymmetric LICs to supply energy density, power density, and cyclic stability higher than those of LIBs and supercapacitors. Recently, research on LIC anodes has been focused on imparting fast-rate charge-transport kinetics to the active materials. Considering that the power density of an LIC is dominated by the nature of the anode, inferior electrical conductivity and lithium-ion diffusion kinetics of the anode hinder the realization of ultrafast rate capabilities (retention of a high specific capacity even during ultrafast charge–discharge and long-term cycling). Therefore, innovative anode materials are required to achieve reversible electron transfer and lithium-ion diffusion kinetics under ultrafast charge–discharge conditions.

The hybridization of high-capacity transition metal oxides with conductive carbon matrices is a promising method for improving electrical conductivity and structural stability. Su et al. [10] reported the use of two-dimensional carbon-coated graphene/metal oxide hybrids as high-performance anode materials. The encapsulation of a metal oxide with a conductive graphene layer can enhance the rate of electron transport into the metal oxide particles and improve their structural stability by accommodating their volume expansion during cycling. Although these carbon coating layers improve the rate capability and cyclic stability of transition metal oxide-based anodes, the interfacial layers introduced between the transition metal oxide and electrolyte can function as an energy barrier that hinder lithium-ion diffusion and electron transfer compared to direct contact with the electrolyte. However, the introduction of an interfacial layer thermodynamically deteriorates ionic diffusion and electron transfer. Therefore, unconventional coating configurations should be investigated, both in terms of morphology and surface chemistry, to minimize ionic/electronic transport degradation, which results in changes in the charge transport kinetics under ultrafast charge–discharge conditions.

In this study, we demonstrated a carbon nano dot-anchored thin film coating on cobalt vanadium oxide (CVO) particles via hydrothermal and carbonization processes. The carbon nano dots were rigidly connected via a thin carbon film on the surface of the CVO microparticles. Moreover, the boron-doped carbon chemistry of the carbon nano dot anchored thin film coating induced ultrafast-rate electron transfer inside the CVO particles, which allowed reversible electron transfer even under ultrafast charge–discharge conditions. This unique carbon-coating configuration featured nanoscale surface roughness, which expanded the contact area of the functional groups on the coating layer. This maximized the lithium-ion affinity at the interface compared with that of a film-like carbon-coating configuration. The developed strategy, which merges the morphological and chemical effects on carbon coating layers, shows promise for use in synthesis of high-performance anodes, especially for ultrafast LICs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of bare CVO, carbon nano dot-anchored thin film coated CVO (C-CVO), and boron-doped carbon nano dot-anchored thin film coated CVO (BC-CVO)
ce:section.ce:para: Bare CVO was synthesized using a coprecipitation method. First, 0.2 M ammonium metavanadate (NH4VO3; Sigma-Aldrich) and 0.1 M cobalt (ⅠⅠ) nitrate hexahydrate (Co(NO3)2·6H2O; Sigma-Aldrich) were separately dissolved in deionized water by using a magnetic stirring bar. After homogeneous mixing, each solution was poured into a beaker and stirred for 12 h at 50 °C. After the completion of coprecipitation reactions, the solution was centrifuged at 6000 rpm and washed with deionized water. The washed sediment was dried in an oven at 50 °C, resulting in a brown CVO powder. To prepare C-CVO, 0.3 wt% urea (CH4N2O; Sigma-Aldrich) and 1 wt% CVO powder were dissolved in deionized water by using a magnetic stirring bar. After homogeneous mixing, the solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and was heated to 180 °C for 3 h. After the completion of hydrothermal reactions, the solution was centrifuged at 6000 rpm and washed with deionized water. The sediment was dried in an oven at 80 °C, resulting in a dark brown urea-coated CVO powder. Subsequently, carbonization was conducted in a tube-type furnace at 900 °C for 2 h in an argon atmosphere, resulting in a black C-CVO powder. In the case of BC-CVO, 0.3 wt% boric acid (H3BO3; Sigma-Aldrich) was also added to the hydrothermal bath, and all subsequent processes were the same as those for C-CVO.

The crystal structures of the as-synthesized samples were investigated by X-ray diffraction (XRD; Rigaku D/Max-2500 V). The XRD spectra were obtained by irradiating a Cu K α X-ray source in the 2θ range of 10°−90°. The thermal weight variations of the samples were analyzed by thermogravimetric analysis (TGA; Shimadzu DTG-60H). The morphologies of the samples were examined by performing field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM; Hitachi SU8010). High-magnification morphology and transmission images were obtained using transmission electron microscopy (TEM; Jeol NEOARM). Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) was performed during TEM. The chemical binding states of the samples were investigated by conducting X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS; Escalab 250Xi).

To investigate the lithium-ion storage performance, three types of electrodes were fabricated by casting different slurries onto copper foils with thicknesses of 9 μm (MTI Korea). The slurries were composed of 10 wt% conductive carbon additive (carbon black, Mitsubishi Chemical), 10 wt% binder (polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF); Arkema), and 80 wt% active material (bare CVO, C-CVO, or BC-CVO) dissolved in a N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP) solvent. The as-prepared slurries were casted onto the copper foils and dried in an oven at 100 °C for 12 h. Then, the dried electrodes were roll-pressed and punched to produce circular electrodes (diameter: 13 mm) for coin-cell (CR2032, Wellcos) assemblies (the active material mass was fixed at ∼1.0 mg/cm2 for all anodes). The cells were assembled by stacking the fabricated circular electrodes as a working electrode; a polypropylene membrane (Celgard 2400) was used as a separator, and lithium metal foil (Honjo) was utilized as a counter electrode, with an electrolyte consisting of 1 M LiPF6 in EC:DEC (1:1; Enchem) in an argon-filled glovebox. The galvanostatic charge–discharge characteristics of the coin cells were evaluated by employing a potentiostat/galvanostat (WonATech). The performance of the LIC full-cell was evaluated by replacing the lithium metal with an AC cathode. The AC cathode was fabricated by casting slurry onto an aluminum foil with a thickness of 10 μm (MTI Korea). The cathode slurry was composed of 80 wt% AC (Power Carbon Technology Co., Ltd.), 10 wt% conductive carbon additive (carbon black; Mitsubishi Chemical), and 10 wt% binder (PVDF; Arkema) in an NMP solvent (the active material mass was ∼1.0 mg/cm2). Before full-cell assembly, the anodes were pre-lithiated by prolonged contact with the lithium metal foil through electrolyte mediation. The pre-lithiated anodes (bare CVO and BC-CVO) were then assembled with AC cathodes, similar to a coin cell assembly.

Carbon nano dot-anchored thin films were uniformly coated onto the CVO surface via hydrothermal and carbonization processes. Unlike conventional flat film coatings, carbon nano dot-anchored thin film coatings not only alleviate the volume expansion during repetitive lithium-ion insertion/extraction, but also promote accelerated charge transport for ultrafast charge–discharge processes. Moreover, boron doping into the sp2-hybridized carbon lattice in the carbon nano dot-anchored thin film can simultaneously enhance the electrical conductivity and lithium-ion affinity, thereby retaining a high specific capacity even under ultrafast charge–discharge processes [11]. Fig. 1 a shows the XRD patterns of bare CVO, C-CVO, and BC-CVO. Bare CVO exhibits crystalline peaks at ∼ 11.58°, 20.08°, 23.16°, 27.92°, and 29.62°, which correspond to the (110), (111), (220), (130), and (312) planes of the CoV2O6 hydrate, respectively (JCPDS #77 − 0485) [12]. Bare CVO was synthesized via a coprecipitation reaction between a cobalt precursor (Co(NO3)2·6H2O) and vanadium precursor (NH4VO3) (molar ratio: 1:2) in water solvent, which produced CoV2O6 hydrate as follows: Co(NO3)2·6H2O+2NH4VO3 → CoV2O6·4H2O+2NH4NO3 + 2H2O. C-CVO and BC-CVO exhibit common crystalline peaks at ∼ 26.95°, 29.30°, 30.85°, 34.96°, 35.73°, 43.27°, 57.85°, 61.10°, and 62.86°, which correspond to the (211), (002), (040), (122), (311), (042), (511), (004), and (442) planes of Co3V2O8, respectively (JCPDS #74-1487) [13]. During the carbonization process, the CoV2O6 hydrate changed from a zigzag arrangement to a layered array via the de-intercalation of water molecules located at the vertex oxygen, thereby forming vertex oxygen-shared CoO6 octahedral and VO4 tetrahedral units. This layered crystal structure of Co3V2O8 induced synergistic effects into the lithium-ion storage mechanism as follows. The CoO6 layer provided lithium-ion storage sites via the conversion reaction of cobalt (2Li+ + 2e− + CoO→Li2O+Co), whereas the VO4 layer contributed to lithium −ion storage via intercalation between the layers. Fig. 1b depicts the TGA profiles of bare CVO, C-CVO, and BC-CVO within the temperatures range of 25–900 ℃ in air. Bare CVO exhibits a two-step weight loss profile, which corresponds to the evaporation of adsorbed H2O (105−160 °C) and de-intercalation of crystal H2O located at the vertex oxygen of CoV2O6 (180−280 °C) [14]. In contrast, C-CVO and BC-CVO exhibit small weight changes until the end of heating at 900 ℃ (98.7 % and 99.7 % for C-CVO and BC-CVO, respectively), demonstrating the thermal stability of the Co3V2O8 crystals. Therefore, the layered crystal structure of the binary metal oxides in C-CVO and BC-CVO can enhance the fast-rated lithium-ion diffusion kinetics through the interlayered channels, suitable for ultrafast LIC anodes.

Fig. 2 a−f present the SEM images of bare CVO (Fig. 2a and d), C-CVO (Fig. 2b and e), and BC-CVO (Fig. 2c and f). Bare CVO exhibits micro-rod-shaped particles with a length of ∼4.8–11.2 μm (Fig. 2a). The enlarged SEM image of bare CVO (Fig. 2d) reveals a smooth surface morphology without any specific particles. In contrast, C-CVO and BC-CVO exhibit polygonal plate morphologies with reduced thickness (length: ∼3.1–6.8 μm and thickness: ∼0.14–0.27 μm) compared to that of bare CVO (Fig. 2b and c). The morphologies of C-CVO and BC-CVO originate from the exfoliation of oxide layers composed of CoO6 octahedra and VO4 tetrahedra due to the de-intercalation of water molecules from vertex oxygen during carbonization. C-CVO and BC-CVO present rough surfaces covered with nano dots (∼27.9–83.7 nm) solidly linked by a carbon thin film (Fig. 2e and f). To elucidate the carbon nano dot-based coating structure, TEM images of bare CVO (Fig. 3 a) and BC-CVO (Fig. 3e) were compared. BC-CVO exhibits translucent micro-plates, whereas bare CVO is characterized by opaque micro-rods. These TEM images support the exfoliated micro-plate morphology of BC-CVO, whereas bare CVO features a thick micro-rod morphology, as confirmed by the SEM images. The enlarged TEM images of bare CVO (Fig. 3b and c) do not show any specific substances, confirming the presence of a pure CoV2O6 hydrate phase without any specific alloy in the bulk. Additionally, the Fast Fourier transform (FFT) pattern of bare CVO (Fig. 3d) reveals a polycrystalline structure with specific lattice spots, corresponding to the (201), (
3
¯
12), and (110) planes of CoV2O6 [15]. The foggy ring pattern is attributed to the presence of carbon residues from the cobalt and vanadium precursors during the low-temperature (50 ℃) coprecipitation process. In contrast, BC-CVO exhibits a compact distribution of nano dots on the CVO surface (Fig. 3f). The scanning TEM image of BC-CVO (inset of Fig. 3f) shows a uniform nano dot anchored coating morphology (nano dot scale: 20–80 nm). The enlarged TEM image in the inset of Fig. 3g reveals a graphitic carbon thin film coating layer with a thickness of ∼9 nm between the nano dots; the lattice spacing of ∼0.34 nm corresponds to the distance between the hexagonal planes of graphite. The FFT pattern of BC-CVO exhibits a clear polycrystalline structure indicating the (122), (442), (042), and (002) planes of Co3V2O8 (Fig. 3h) [16]. Additionally, the TEM images of C-CVO in Fig. S1 show uniform carbon nano dots (∼15.5–80.5 nm) on its surface, which is highly consistent with the surface characteristics of BC-CVO. Further, TEM-EDS mapping images of BC-CVO confirmed the presence of carbon, boron, oxygen, cobalt, and vanadium along the particles (Fig. 4 ). In particular, the uniform distributions of carbon and boron indicated the successful doping of boron into the carbon nano dot-based coating layer. This carbon nano dot-anchored thin film coating morphology is capable of converting the charge transport behaviors at the CVO/electrolyte interface as follows. (i) The carbon nano dot buffer, which is chemically linked through the carbon thin film on the CVO surface, effectively tolerates structural deformation upon repetitive lithium-ion transport. (ii) The carbon thin film region alleviates the rate limitation of lithium-ion transport by shortening the diffusion channels between the CVO and electrolyte compared to those of the bulk carbon coating. (iii) The nano-scale surface roughness, which is generated by the nano dot anchored coating architecture, expands the contact area of the functional groups on the coating layer, thereby maximizing the lithium-ion affinity at the electrode/electrolyte interface.

Fig. 5 shows the C1s, O1s, Co2p, and V2p XPS profiles of bare CVO, C-CVO, and BC-CVO. In the C1s spectra, all samples feature common chemical bonds at 282.0, 284.5, and 286.2 eV, which correspond to C–Metal, C–C, and C–OH bonds, respectively (Fig. 5a). The C1s XPS spectrum of bare CVO is attributed to the carbon residue from the precursors. Considering that C-CVO and BC-CVO undergo high-temperature processes (900 ℃) to remove all possible C–Metal bonds that may originate from the carbon residues, the presence of strong C–Metal peaks in C-CVO and BC-CVO ensures a robust chemical linkage between the carbon coating layer and CVO surface. Moreover, C-CVO and BC-CVO exhibit a common C=O bond centered at ∼288.0 eV, formed by the thermal decomposition of urea during the hydrothermal and carbonization processes. Urea, added as carbon coating source, is composed of a carbonyl (C=O) group with two C-bound amino (–NH2) groups. Thus, urea is prone to adsorption onto the CVO surface via the establishment of hydrogen bonds between the –NH2 and –OH groups on the CVO surface, which formed a urea thin film on the CVO surface during the initial hydrothermal process (Fig. 5f) [17]. As the hydrothermal process continued, the remaining urea self-aggregated on the urea thin film owing to its hydrophobic effect, leading to consecutive nucleation on the aggregates [18]. During the carbonization process, the urea coating layer, in the form of nano dots in the thin-film configuration, was transformed into a sp2-bonded carbon lattice structure with carbonyl functional groups. Carbonyl functional groups have significant polarity because of the large electronegativity difference between carbon (2.55) and oxygen (3.44), which facilitates ionic diffusion at the CVO/electrolyte interfaces. BC-CVO exhibits an extra peak at ∼283.4 eV, corresponding to the C–B bond. During the hydrothermal process, boron atoms in boric acid combined with oxygen atoms in urea, thereby incorporating the boron atoms into the urea nano dot-based thin film coating layer. As carbonization proceeded, C–B bonds formed in hexagonal C–C lattices, owing to the similar covalent radii of boron (∼84 pm) and carbon (∼73 pm). This boron-doping configuration increased the electrical conductivity at the CVO surface by shifting the Fermi level to the conduction band of the carbon atoms [19]. Thus, the boron-doped carbon coating layer can boost the electrical contacts between the CVO particles in the electrode and provide faster electron transfer even under ultrafast charge–discharge processes compared to bare CVO and C-CVO. To investigate the effect of the amount of boron on the electrochemical performance, we measured the specific capacity variations with increasing current density of three types of BC-CVO electrodes fabricated with different concentrations (0.16, 0.3, and 0.5 wt%) of boron-doping precursors (denoted as 0.16 wt% BC-CVO, 0.30 wt% BC-CVO, and 0.50 wt% BC-CVO, respectively) (Fig. S2). In the results, 0.30 wt% BC-CVO exhibits the highest specific capacity at all current densities among the considered electrodes. Significantly, all three electrodes show enhanced performance compared to that of bare CVO, demonstrating the effects of boron-doping on the speed of the charge transport kinetics under ultrafast charge–discharge conditions. Relatively small amounts of boric acid (0.16 wt% BC-CVO) may make incorporating abundant boron atoms into the carbon nano dot-based thin film coating layer difficult, resulting in specific capacity degradation at high current densities. Relatively large amounts of boric acid (0.50 wt% BC-CVO) may produce excessive compounds (boric oxides) during the hydrothermal and carbonization process, which may act as an energy barrier to ion diffusion between the CVO and electrolyte. Fig. 5b shows the O1s spectra of bare CVO, C-CVO, and BC-CVO. All samples share common peaks at ∼528.0, 530.05, and 532.0 eV, corresponding to lattice oxygen, metal oxide, and C–O, respectively. The highest ratios of metal oxide peaks originated from the Co–O and V–O bonds in the CVO particles. Unlike C-CVO and BC-CVO, bare CVO shows an additional chemical bond at ∼533.0 eV, corresponding to H2O (a proportion of 3.3 % in the O1s XPS spectra). This finding indicates intermolecular chemical bonds between the octahedral cobalt oxide and tetrahedral vanadium oxide layers in bare CVO via H2O molecules. Thus, the absence of the H2O peak in C-CVO and BC-CVO verifies the deintercalation of water molecules during the carbonization process [20]. The Co2p XPS spectra of all samples feature four common peaks: one each corresponding to Co2p1/2 and Co2p3/2, and two corresponding to Cosat (Fig. 5c). The Co2p1/2 and Co2p3/2 peaks can be deconvoluted into the oxidation states of Co3+ (∼780.6 and 796.5 eV) and Co2+ (∼782.3 and 797.9 eV), respectively. The prominent Co3+ and Co2+ states confirm the presence of cobalt ions in the octahedral coordination and surface regions, respectively, corroborating the presence of layered octahedral cobalt oxides (CoO6) in all samples. Similarly, the V2p XPS profiles of all samples feature two separate peaks corresponding to V2p1/2 and V2p3/2 (Fig. 5d). The V2p1/2 and V2p3/2 peaks can be deconvoluted into the oxidation states of V4+ (∼515.9 and 523.5 eV) and V5+ (∼517.1 and 524.5 eV), respectively [21]. Most V5+ states confirm the pentahedral and tetrahedral coordination of V ions, which correspond to the VO5 (bare CVO) and VO4 units (C-CVO and BC-CVO), respectively. Therefore, the Co2p and V2p XPS results reveal binary metal coordination states with octahedral cobalt oxides and pentahedral vanadium oxides in the case of bare CVO, consistent with the crystal structure of CoV2O6. Similarly, the Co2p and V2p XPS results of C-CVO and BC-CVO confirm the presence of binary metal coordination states with octahedral cobalt oxides and tetrahedral vanadium oxides, respectively, consistent with the crystal structure of Co3V2O8. In the calculated C1s bonding proportions of all samples, only BC-CVO shows a noticeable C−B bonding ratio of 4.3 %, demonstrating definite boron-doping into the sp2-hybridized carbon lattice structure (Fig. 5e). Moreover, BC-CVO and C-CVO show C=O bonding ratios of 9.4 % and 9.0 %, respectively, proving the presence of carbonyl functional groups on the unique carbon coating layer (Fig. 5e). Thus, the chemical bonding states of BC-CVO reveal (i) boron doping into the unique carbon coating thin film layer, (ii) the presence of carbonyl functional groups on the carbon coating layer, and (iii) the formation of chemical linkages between the carbon coating layer and CVO particles. The first effect can enhance the electrical conductivity of the LIC electrode even at an ultrahigh current density, the second can promote lithium-ion diffusivity at the CVO/electrolyte interface, and the third can enhance the interconnected electron transfer between the CVO particles (besides the conductive carbon additive), while maintaining structural integration during the pulverization of CVO microparticles.

Fig. 6 (a–c) present the voltage profiles of bare CVO, C-CVO, and BC-CVO, respectively, at a current density of 100 mA/g for the 1st, 10th, and 100th charge–discharge processes. During the first discharge process, bare CVO exhibits a steep voltage drop of ∼1.28 V, followed by a voltage plateau at ∼1.18 V (Fig. 6a). This first voltage plateau is associated with the formation of a solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) layer at the surface of the CVO particles. A second voltage plateau is observed from ∼0.89 V to 0.64 V, which originated from the conversion reaction of Co (CoO→metallic Co) [22,23]. Subsequently, the voltage gradually decreases to ∼0.05 V during the completion of the full discharge process, owing to further lithium-ion intercalation into the interstitial sites between the CoO6 and VO4 crystal units. However, a significant specific capacity loss is observed during the first charge process, owing to the formation of an unavoidable SEI layer during this discharge process. After prolonged cycling until the 10th and 100th cycles, bare CVO exhibits substantial specific capacity degradation during both the charge and discharge processes (discharge capacity: 417.9 mAh/g at the 100th cycle). This capacity irreversibility of bare CVO was caused by the structural collapse of the CVO microparticles, which led to the growth of an additional SEI layer at the freshly exposed CVO surface. Eventually, the frequent formation of thick SEI layers induced electrical disconnection between the CVO particles, eliminating possible lithium-ion storage sites. In the first discharge profiles of C-CVO and BC-CVO (Fig. 6b and c), similar voltage-drop profiles are observed, featuring two stepped voltage plateaus (∼0.86 and 0.47 V) and a gradual voltage drop region (from ∼ 0.33 to 0.05 V) before the full discharge process has been completed. Both C-CVO and BC-CVO exhibit first voltage plateaus at ∼0.86 V, which are delayed compared to that of bare CVO (∼1.18 V), signifying a different SEI formation potential due to the variations in the interface chemistry of the carbon nano dot-anchored thin film coating layer [24,25]. Even though the carbon nano dot anchored thin film layer effectively prevents particle disintegration, redox irreversibility caused by inferior electron transfer inside the particles still hinders the cyclic stability of C-CVO (discharge capacity: 649.1 mAh/g at the 100th cycle) (Fig. 6b). In contrast, BC-CVO shows an almost intact discharge capacity even after 100 cycles (discharge capacity: 951.8 mAh/g at the 100th cycle) (Fig. 6c). In Fig. 6d, BC-CVO exhibits a discharge capacity retention of 90.1 %, whereas bare CVO and C-CVO exhibit inferior retention of 37.8 and 63.7 %, respectively. In the first cycle, bare CVO displays the highest initial specific capacity of 1886.5 mAh/g, whereas those of C-CVO and BC-CVO are 1611.7 mAh/g and 1711.8 mAh/g, respectively, due to the excessive formation of SEI layer on bare CVO surface. Additionally, BC-CVO exhibits the highest initial and second Coulombic efficiencies (CEs) of 63.99 % and 96.86 %, respectively, whereas those of bare CVO are 59.01 % and 90.43 % and those of C-CVO 62.72 % and 94.67 %, respectively. These findings further confirm that the electrochemical reversibility of BC-CVO is superior to those of bare CVO and C-CVO due to the electrically active carbon nano dot anchored thin film coating layer of the former, which promotes simultaneous ion and electron transport into CVO particles. This trend in CE is maintained even under repeated ultrafast charge–discharge cycles, as shown in Fig. 6f. The boron-doping in the carbon coating layer enhanced the electrical conductivity into the interior of the CVO particles by linking the electrical pathways between the CVO particles and conductive carbon additives, leading to the rate capability of BC-CVO being significantly higher than those of bare CVO and C-CVO. This is because the rate capability of electrodes is dominated by the electron transfer kinetics at high current densities. Fig. 6e presents the discharge capacity variations of all samples according to the applied stepped current density. In the first cycle, bare CVO shows a specific capacity higher than those of C-CVO and BC-CVO due to excessive SEI formation, which is highly consistent with the results in Fig. 6a–c. Bare CVO exhibits a drastic capacity fading as the current density increases from 100 to 4000 mA/g (79.3 mAh/g at 4000 mA/g), rendering it applicable in LIC electrodes. C-CVO has capacity retention of 252.1 mAh/g at 4000 mA/g, which is better than that of bare CVO, owing to the introduction of an electrically conductive carbon coating layer in the form of a carbon nano dot-anchored thin film [26]. Furthermore, BC-CVO exhibits remarkable capacity retention even at 4000 mA/g (429.6 mAh/g at 4000 mA/g), owing to the superior electron and ion transfer kinetics due to the synergistic effects of boron-doping and the carbon nano dot-anchored thin film configuration. To demonstrate the enhanced lithium-ion diffusion kinetics of BC-CVO, the galvanostatic intermittent titration technique (GITT) was used for all electrodes (Fig. S3). The GITT method was conducted by inducing repetitive pulses (for each, a constant current of 300 mA/g was applied for 15 min, followed by resting for 30 min) during the charge–discharge process. Fig. S3a–f display the voltage profiles of (a and d) bare CVO, (b and e) C-CVO, and (c and f) BC-CVO measured by utilizing the GITT. The DLi was calculated using the followed equation [27]:
(1)
D Li
=
4
L 2
×
( △
E s
/ △
E t
)
/ π τ
where L is the diffusion length (m), which is equivalent to the average CVO particle length; △Es and △Et are the steady-state and transient-state voltage changes (V) at constant current, respectively; and τ is the rest time (s). Accordingly, the DLi values of all electrodes in the middle of the charge–discharge process are summarized in Fig. S3g. In these results, BC-CVO shows the highest DLi values during both the charge and discharge processes (∼1.45 × 10–10 and ∼4.91 × 10–11 cm2/s, respectively), whereas those of bare CVO are ∼ 7.34 × 10–11 and ∼ 1.91 × 10–11 cm2/s and those of C-CVO are ∼ 1.14 × 10–10 and ∼ 2.58 × 10–11 cm2/s, respectively. These findings demonstrate the ultrafast lithium-ion diffusion capability of BC-CVO through the unique coating configuration of the carbon nano-dot anchored thin film morphology with enhanced carbonyl functional groups. Consequently, this ultrafast-rate capability of BC-CVO enables it to outperform previous CVO-based materials, demonstrating the ultrafast charge transport kinetics achievable using the boron-doped carbon nano dot anchored thin film coating strategy (Fig. S4 and Table S2) [28–33]. Additionally, these synergistic effects of the boron-doping and unique coating configuration on the ultrafast electrochemical performance were examined by performing long cycle measurements at a high current density of 2000 mA/g (Fig. 6f). Before the ultrafast charge–discharge cycles, all electrodes were cycled at 100 mA/g for three cycles to form a stable SEI layer. In the results, bare CVO exhibits the highest initial specific capacity of 1971.9 mAh/g, whereas those of C-CVO and BC-CVO are 1421.9 mAh/g and 1536.7 mAh/g, respectively. Bare CVO exhibits rapid discharge capacity fading from early cycles, eventually reaching a discharge capacity of 140.7 mAh/g at the 1000th cycle. Although C-CVO has capacity retention of 289.5 mAh/g at the 1000th cycle, which is better than that of bare CVO, it is unsuitable for LIC electrode applications that require high specific capacity retention after prolonged ultrafast charge–discharge cycles. In this regard, BC-CVO shows optimal ultrafast longevity with superior capacity retention of 534.3 mAh/g at the 1000th cycle.

The superior ultrafast charge–discharge characteristics of the BC-CVO electrode prompted us to perform a full-cell test in combination with the AC cathode. Fig. 7 a and b depict the full-cell configurations of the bare CVO and BC-CVO electrodes, respectively (denoted as bare CVO//AC and BC-CVO//AC, respectively). Before the full-cell assembly, both the bare CVO and BC-CVO anodes were pre-lithiated by prolonged contact with lithium metal foils through electrolyte mediation. After the pre-lithiation process, both anodes were assembled into full cells, as shown in Fig. 7a and b. Fig. 7c and d present the galvanostatic charge–discharge (GCD) curves of the bare CVO//AC and BC-CVO//AC full cells, respectively, at different current densities. Notably, BC-CVO//AC exhibits an impedance resistance (IR) drop of ∼0.6 V, whereas bare CVO//AC exhibits a drop of ∼0.1 V at 0.5 A/g, reflecting the reduced electrical resistance of the LIC full cell. Considering that only the working electrode was placed differently in the full cells, this finding implies a significant reduction in the electrical resistance of the BC-CVO electrode compared to that of the bare CVO electrode. Furthermore, the BC-CVO//AC full cell exhibits much longer charge–discharge times at high current densities (2.0–5.0 A/g), indicating the suitability of BC-CVO electrodes as LIC anodes (Fig. 7e and f). Although the IR drop of bare CVO//AC substantially accelerates with increasing current density, BC-CVO//AC still exhibits a small IR drop even under ultrafast charge–discharge conditions. The specific capacitance and capacity of each LIC full cell were calculated based on the GCD results (Fig. 8 a). At all current densities, BC-CVO//AC exhibits energy storage capabilities (134.3F/g and 36.8 mAh/g at 0.5 A/g and 55.2F/g and 15.1 mAh/g at 5.0 A/g) higher than those of bare CVO//AC (80.5F/g and 22.1 mAh/g at 0.5 A/g and 13.1F/g and 3.6 mAh/g at 5.0 A/g), indicating the ultrafast rate capability of BC-CVO//AC full cells. To investigate the effects of the anode:cathode mass ratio on the performance of LIC full cells, we compared the energy storage performance of full cells fabricated with anode:cathode mass ratios of 1:1 and 1:2for bare CVO//AC and BC-CVO//AC, respectively (Fig. S5). The mass ratio of 1:1 yielded higher energy storage performance with enhanced specific capacitance and specific capacity at increasing current density compared to those obtained when the mass ratio was 1:2. Thus, we identified 1:1 as the optimal anode:cathode mass ratio in this study. Ragone plots were compared to investigate the variations in energy density with respect to the applied power density of the LIC full cells (Fig. 8b). The energy and power densities were calculated using the following equations [34]:
(2)
Power density
W / kg
= Δ V × I / m ×
1
000
(3)
Δ V =
V max
+
V min
/ 2
(4)
Energy density
Wh / kg
=
power density
× t / 36 00
where ΔV, I, m, and t are the voltage window, current, active material mass of the anode and cathode, and discharge time, respectively. Bare CVO//AC exhibits a steep reduction in energy density as the power density increases (44.7 and 7.3 Wh/kg at 487.9 and 4879.5 W/kg, respectively), whereas BC-CVO//AC exhibits decent retention of energy density (74.6 and 30.6 Wh/kg at 492.3 and 4673.1 W/kg, respectively), confirming the sustainable operation of BC-CVO electrodes in LIC full-cell settings. Furthermore, these values of BC-CVO//AC exceed those of previous CVO-based full cells [35–40]. The suitability of the BC-CVO//AC LIC full cell was effectively visualized by powering a 1.5 V LED with the fabricated full cells (Fig. 8c–e). Before operating the LEDs, both full cells were fully charged by energizing at a constant current until the cells reached 4.0 V. The BC-CVO//AC full cell illuminated the LED for much longer (∼30 min) than the bare CVO//AC cell did (∼4 min), owing to the higher energy density of the BC-CVO electrode. In particular, the initial illumination intensity of the LED powered by BC-CVO//AC is higher than that of the LED powered by bare CVO//AC, verifying the improved power density of the BC-CVO electrode (Fig. 8e). This is consistent with the characteristics of the BC-CVO electrode, such as the reduced IR drop, high specific capacitance at high current density, and high energy density at high power density. Thus, the synergistic effects of the unique coating configuration with carbon nano dot-anchored thin-film morphology and boron-doping can alter the electron transfer kinetics of the CVO microparticles. This can solve the original issues originating from the inferior structural stability and poor electrical conductivity of micrometer-scale CVO particles, facilitating their effective application in LIC anodes.

A carbon nano dot-anchored thin film layer was introduced onto the micrometer-scale CVO surface via hydrothermal and carbonization processes. The introduction of a carbon nano dot-anchored thin film coating layer induced a robust chemical linkage between the CVO particles and conductive carbon additive, which formed electron transfer pathways throughout the electrode. Additionally, this unique coating configuration generated nanoscale surface roughness and expanded the contact area between the carbonyl functional groups and electrolyte, thereby maximizing the lithium-ion affinity at the electrode/electrolyte interface. Moreover, boron doping into the sp2-hybridized carbon lattice in the coating layer activated fast-rate electron transfer inside the particles, enabling reversible electron transfer even under ultrafast charge–discharge conditions. Accordingly, BC-CVO anodes exhibited enhanced specific capacity at high current density, excellent cyclic stability, and ultrafast longevity compared to those of bare CVO and C-CVO anodes. In particular, the full cells fabricated with BC-CVO as the anode and AC as the cathode exhibited a significantly decreased IR drop, higher specific capacitance at high current density, higher energy density at high power density, and higher illumination intensity and operating time than those of bare CVO//AC cells. In summary, these results indicate the suitability of BC-CVO as a high-performance LIC anode material. Thus, this approach can enable the incorporation of micrometer-scale oxide materials with high-capacitance properties into the anodes, thereby increasing the energy density during the ultrafast charge–discharge of LICs.

Ha-Na Jang: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Myeong-Hun Jo: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Hyo-Jin Ahn: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Nano & Material Technology Development Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF, South Korea) funded by Ministry of Science and ICT (2021M3H4A3A02086102) and the Industrial Strategic Technology Development Program (20024261, Development of thick film electrode and cell manufacturing technology for high-performance lithium iron phosphate battery with energy density of over 200 Wh/kg) funded By the Ministry of Trade, Industry & Energy (MOTIE, South Korea).
